question: A production server is experiencing high load averages (15+) but CPU utilization stays under 20%. 'top' shows many processes in D (uninterruptible sleep) state. What is the most likely root cause?
options: The system is CPU-bound and needs more cores
Processes are waiting on I/O operations (disk or NFS), causing I/O wait
The scheduler is misconfigured and needs tuning via nice values
OOM killer is throttling processes
correct_answer: Processes are waiting on I/O operations (disk or NFS), causing I/O wait

question: You run 'iostat -x 1' and notice that a disk has an average queue size (avgqu-sz) of 32 and %util is at 100%. The await time is 450ms. What does this indicate?
options: The disk is idle and performing optimally
The disk is saturated with high latency; requests are queuing excessively
The disk controller firmware needs updating
The filesystem is corrupted and needs fsck
correct_answer: The disk is saturated with high latency; requests are queuing excessively

question: A Java application's response times have degraded. You notice the system's swap usage is at 6GB despite 32GB physical RAM. 'vmstat 1' shows high si/so values. What is happening and what should you check first?
options: The kernel is proactively caching swap; this is normal behavior
The system is thrashing — memory is overcommitted and pages are constantly swapping in/out; check per-process RSS with 'ps aux --sort=-rss'
Swap is being used as additional RAM intentionally; increase swap to 12GB
The Java application has a file descriptor leak causing swap usage
correct_answer: The system is thrashing — memory is overcommitted and pages are constantly swapping in/out; check per-process RSS with 'ps aux --sort=-rss'

question: You need to identify which process is consuming the most disk I/O on a Linux server. Which tool gives per-process I/O statistics including read/write bytes per second?
options: top
iotop
vmstat
netstat
correct_answer: iotop

question: After deploying a new application, 'dmesg' shows repeated 'Out of memory: Kill process' messages. The server has 64GB RAM but the application is configured with -Xmx48g. What kernel parameter controls how aggressively the OOM killer acts, and what value disables memory overcommit?
options: vm.swappiness=0 disables OOM killing entirely
vm.overcommit_memory=2 disables overcommit; the kernel will reject allocations exceeding physical RAM + swap
kernel.panic=1 prevents OOM situations
vm.oom_kill_allocating_task=0 stops the OOM killer permanently
correct_answer: vm.overcommit_memory=2 disables overcommit; the kernel will reject allocations exceeding physical RAM + swap

question: What is the difference between the 'resident set size' (RSS) and 'virtual memory size' (VSZ) of a process as shown by 'ps aux'?
options: RSS includes swap usage; VSZ does not
RSS is the physical memory currently in use by the process; VSZ is the total virtual address space including mapped but not yet allocated pages
VSZ is always smaller than RSS because it excludes shared libraries
They are identical on systems without swap enabled
correct_answer: RSS is the physical memory currently in use by the process; VSZ is the total virtual address space including mapped but not yet allocated pages

question: A production database server has vm.swappiness set to 60 (default). The DBA complains about unpredictable latency spikes. What tuning would you recommend and why?
options: Set vm.swappiness=100 to maximize I/O throughput
Set vm.swappiness=1 or lower to strongly prefer reclaiming page cache over swapping out application memory, reducing latency spikes
Set vm.swappiness=60 is optimal for all workloads; investigate elsewhere
Disable swap entirely with swapoff; this is always the best approach for databases
correct_answer: Set vm.swappiness=1 or lower to strongly prefer reclaiming page cache over swapping out application memory, reducing latency spikes

question: What does the 'dirty_ratio' and 'dirty_background_ratio' kernel parameters control in Linux?
options: The percentage of corrupted filesystem blocks before fsck triggers
The threshold of dirty (modified in page cache but not written to disk) pages before processes are blocked vs. when background writeback starts
The ratio of user-space to kernel-space memory allocation
The maximum percentage of swap that can be used before OOM triggers
correct_answer: The threshold of dirty (modified in page cache but not written to disk) pages before processes are blocked vs. when background writeback starts

question: You need to trace all system calls made by a running process with PID 4521 without stopping it. Which command would you use?
options: strace -p 4521
ltrace --pid 4521
gdb attach 4521
perf record -p 4521
correct_answer: strace -p 4521

question: A process is stuck and not responding but hasn't crashed. 'strace -p <pid>' shows it's blocked on a futex() call. What does this suggest?
options: The process is performing a DNS lookup
The process is waiting on a userspace lock (mutex/semaphore), likely a deadlock or contention issue
The filesystem is read-only
The process has exceeded its CPU time limit
correct_answer: The process is waiting on a userspace lock (mutex/semaphore), likely a deadlock or contention issue

question: You need to find which files a process has open, including sockets, pipes, and file descriptors. Which command provides this information?
options: fuser
lsof -p <pid>
stat /proc/<pid>
file /proc/<pid>/fd
correct_answer: lsof -p <pid>

question: A server is running out of file descriptors and 'ulimit -n' shows 1024. You need to increase the limit to 65535 for a specific service running under systemd. Where should you configure this?
options: Only in /etc/security/limits.conf under the user's entry
In the systemd unit file using LimitNOFILE=65535, since systemd-managed services do not inherit limits.conf
By running 'ulimit -n 65535' in the service startup script
In /etc/sysctl.conf using fs.file-max=65535
correct_answer: In the systemd unit file using LimitNOFILE=65535, since systemd-managed services do not inherit limits.conf

question: What is the difference between 'systemctl restart' and 'systemctl reload' for a service?
options: They are functionally identical
'restart' stops and starts the service (new PID); 'reload' sends a signal (typically SIGHUP) to re-read config without stopping the service
'reload' always requires a reboot; 'restart' does not
'restart' only works for enabled services; 'reload' works for all
correct_answer: 'restart' stops and starts the service (new PID); 'reload' sends a signal (typically SIGHUP) to re-read config without stopping the service

question: A service is in 'failed' state after a crash. You want it to automatically restart on failure with a 5-second delay. Which systemd unit directives achieve this?
options: AutoRestart=true and Delay=5
Restart=on-failure and RestartSec=5 in the [Service] section
WatchdogSec=5 and FailureAction=restart
ExecRestart=/bin/sleep 5 in the [Unit] section
correct_answer: Restart=on-failure and RestartSec=5 in the [Service] section

question: You need to view the last 200 log entries for the nginx service since today, including kernel messages. Which journalctl command achieves this?
options: journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200 -k
journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200
journalctl --unit=nginx --lines=200 --follow
tail -200 /var/log/syslog | grep nginx
correct_answer: journalctl -u nginx --since today -n 200

question: In systemd, what is the purpose of the 'WantedBy=multi-user.target' directive in the [Install] section?
options: It forces the service to run in single-user mode
It creates a dependency so the service starts when the system reaches multi-user runlevel (runlevel 3 equivalent), i.e., when 'systemctl enable' is run
It limits the service to only run on multi-CPU systems
It prevents the service from starting during boot
correct_answer: It creates a dependency so the service starts when the system reaches multi-user runlevel (runlevel 3 equivalent), i.e., when 'systemctl enable' is run

question: You suspect a network issue between your application server and a database on port 5432. 'ping' works but connections fail intermittently. Which tool would help you diagnose the TCP-level connectivity and path issues?
options: ping -c 10 db-server
traceroute db-server && ss -tnp | grep 5432
nslookup db-server
arp -a db-server
correct_answer: traceroute db-server && ss -tnp | grep 5432

question: The command 'ss -tlnp' shows a process listening on 0.0.0.0:8080 but external clients cannot connect. Firewall rules (iptables -L) show no DROP rules. What else could be blocking external access?
options: The process is only configured for IPv6
A cloud security group, network ACL, or firewalld/nftables rule is blocking inbound traffic on port 8080
The process must listen on 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0
The ss command output is unreliable; use netstat instead
correct_answer: A cloud security group, network ACL, or firewalld/nftables rule is blocking inbound traffic on port 8080

question: What is the difference between iptables chains INPUT, FORWARD, and OUTPUT?
options: INPUT handles all packets; FORWARD and OUTPUT are aliases
INPUT processes packets destined for the local system; FORWARD handles packets being routed through; OUTPUT handles locally generated outbound packets
INPUT handles TCP; FORWARD handles UDP; OUTPUT handles ICMP
They all process the same packets but in different priority order
correct_answer: INPUT processes packets destined for the local system; FORWARD handles packets being routed through; OUTPUT handles locally generated outbound packets

question: You need to capture all DNS queries leaving a server to debug name resolution issues. Which tcpdump command would you use?
options: tcpdump -i any port 53 -nn
tcpdump -i eth0 icmp
tcpdump -i any port 80
tcpdump -i lo port 53
correct_answer: tcpdump -i any port 53 -nn

question: A server has multiple network interfaces. You want a service to only listen on the 10.0.1.5 interface, not on all interfaces. How is this typically achieved?
options: Configure iptables to block other interfaces
Bind the service to 10.0.1.5 instead of 0.0.0.0 in its configuration
Remove all other network interfaces using ip link delete
Set the default gateway to 10.0.1.5
correct_answer: Bind the service to 10.0.1.5 instead of 0.0.0.0 in its configuration

question: The 'ss -s' command shows 15,000 sockets in TIME_WAIT state on a high-traffic web server. What does this indicate and what kernel parameter can help?
options: The server has a memory leak; restart all services
These are closed connections waiting for late packets; enable net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1 to allow reuse of TIME_WAIT sockets for new outbound connections
All 15,000 connections are actively transferring data
Increase the number of network interfaces to distribute the load
correct_answer: These are closed connections waiting for late packets; enable net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1 to allow reuse of TIME_WAIT sockets for new outbound connections

question: You run 'curl -v https://api.internal.company.com' and get 'SSL: certificate subject name does not match target host name'. The IP resolves correctly. What is the issue?
options: The SSL certificate is expired
The certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) or Common Name (CN) does not include 'api.internal.company.com'
The server is using HTTP/2 which is incompatible
The CA bundle is missing on the client
correct_answer: The certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) or Common Name (CN) does not include 'api.internal.company.com'

question: What is the significance of the setuid (SUID) bit on a Linux executable, and why is it a security concern?
options: It allows the file to be executed by any user but with no special privileges
It causes the executable to run with the permissions of the file owner (often root) regardless of who executes it, creating potential privilege escalation risks
It prevents the file from being modified after creation
It encrypts the file contents during execution
correct_answer: It causes the executable to run with the permissions of the file owner (often root) regardless of who executes it, creating potential privilege escalation risks

question: During a security audit, you need to find all SUID binaries on the system. Which command accomplishes this?
options: ls -la /usr/bin | grep suid
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
chmod -R 4000 /
locate --suid
correct_answer: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

question: You need to restrict SSH access to only key-based authentication and disable root login. Which sshd_config directives should you set?
options: AllowPassword yes, PermitRootLogin yes
PasswordAuthentication no, PermitRootLogin no, PubkeyAuthentication yes
AuthenticationMethods password, RootLogin disabled
UseSSHKeys only, DisableRoot true
correct_answer: PasswordAuthentication no, PermitRootLogin no, PubkeyAuthentication yes

question: What does the 'sticky bit' on a directory (e.g., /tmp with permissions drwxrwxrwt) accomplish?
options: It makes the directory read-only for all users
Only the file owner, directory owner, or root can delete or rename files within it, even though others have write permission to the directory
It prevents any new files from being created
It causes files to be automatically encrypted
correct_answer: Only the file owner, directory owner, or root can delete or rename files within it, even though others have write permission to the directory

question: A compromised server had an attacker add a cron job. Where should you check for persistence mechanisms beyond /etc/crontab?
options: Only /etc/crontab needs to be checked
Check /var/spool/cron/crontabs/, /etc/cron.d/, /etc/cron.{daily,weekly,monthly}/, user crontabs, systemd timers, and ~/.bashrc/.profile for backdoors
Only check /tmp for suspicious scripts
Run 'crontab -l' as root only; that shows everything
correct_answer: Check /var/spool/cron/crontabs/, /etc/cron.d/, /etc/cron.{daily,weekly,monthly}/, user crontabs, systemd timers, and ~/.bashrc/.profile for backdoors

question: What is the purpose of SELinux 'enforcing' mode and how does it differ from 'permissive' mode?
options: Enforcing mode logs violations but allows them; permissive mode blocks all access
Enforcing mode actively blocks policy violations and logs them; permissive mode only logs violations without blocking, useful for testing policies
Both modes are identical in behavior
Enforcing mode disables all user access; permissive mode allows root-only access
correct_answer: Enforcing mode actively blocks policy violations and logs them; permissive mode only logs violations without blocking, useful for testing policies

question: You need to create a filesystem on /dev/sdb1, mount it at /data with noexec and nosuid options, and make it persist across reboots. What is the correct sequence?
options: format /dev/sdb1; mount /data; edit /etc/mtab
mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1; mkdir -p /data; mount -o noexec,nosuid /dev/sdb1 /data; add entry to /etc/fstab with options noexec,nosuid
fdisk /dev/sdb1 --format; mountpoint /data; echo mount >> /etc/rc.local
parted /dev/sdb1 mkfs; mount /dev/sdb1 /data; crontab @reboot mount
correct_answer: mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1; mkdir -p /data; mount -o noexec,nosuid /dev/sdb1 /data; add entry to /etc/fstab with options noexec,nosuid

question: A production server shows a filesystem at 100% usage but 'du -sh /*' reports only 60% used. What could explain this discrepancy?
options: The 'du' command is inaccurate on ext4 filesystems
Deleted files are still held open by running processes, consuming space not visible to du; use 'lsof +L1' to find them
The filesystem has bad sectors hiding the actual usage
Reserved blocks for root (typically 5%) account for 40% of disk space
correct_answer: Deleted files are still held open by running processes, consuming space not visible to du; use 'lsof +L1' to find them

question: What is the key difference between ext4 and XFS filesystems in a production Linux environment?
options: ext4 supports only files up to 1GB; XFS supports unlimited file sizes
XFS excels at parallel I/O with large files and cannot be shrunk; ext4 supports both grow and shrink operations and is more versatile for general use
ext4 is deprecated in all modern distributions in favor of XFS
XFS does not support journaling while ext4 does
correct_answer: XFS excels at parallel I/O with large files and cannot be shrunk; ext4 supports both grow and shrink operations and is more versatile for general use

question: You need to extend a logical volume /dev/vg0/lv_data by 50GB and resize the ext4 filesystem online (without unmounting). What commands would you use?
options: lvextend -L +50G /dev/vg0/lv_data && resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data
vgextend +50G vg0 && mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg0/lv_data
fdisk /dev/vg0/lv_data --resize +50G
lvresize -r -L 50G /dev/vg0/lv_data (this shrinks to 50G, not extends by 50G)
correct_answer: lvextend -L +50G /dev/vg0/lv_data && resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data

question: What does the 'inode' represent in a Linux filesystem, and what happens when all inodes are exhausted even though disk space is available?
options: Inodes store file data blocks; exhausting them corrupts existing files
An inode stores metadata (permissions, ownership, timestamps, block pointers) for a file; when inodes are exhausted, no new files or directories can be created despite free disk space
Inodes are network indexes; exhausting them blocks network access
Inodes are only relevant to NFS filesystems
correct_answer: An inode stores metadata (permissions, ownership, timestamps, block pointers) for a file; when inodes are exhausted, no new files or directories can be created despite free disk space

question: A server has a RAID-5 array with 4 disks, and one disk fails. During rebuild with a hot spare, a second disk fails. What happens?
options: The array continues operating normally with degraded performance
The array fails completely and data is lost; RAID-5 can tolerate only one disk failure at a time
The array automatically switches to RAID-1 mode
Data is preserved because the hot spare already has a complete copy
correct_answer: The array fails completely and data is lost; RAID-5 can tolerate only one disk failure at a time

question: You need to write a bash script that processes a CSV file, extracts the 3rd column, sorts unique values, and counts occurrences. Which pipeline accomplishes this?
options: cat file.csv | grep -c column3
awk -F',' '{print $3}' file.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
cut -f3 file.csv | sort | wc -l
sed 's/,/\n/g' file.csv | head -3
correct_answer: awk -F',' '{print $3}' file.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

question: What is the difference between $*, $@, "$*", and "$@" in bash when a script is called with arguments 'arg1' 'arg 2' 'arg3'?
options: They are all identical and interchangeable
"$@" preserves each argument as a separate quoted word (3 args); "$*" combines all arguments into a single string; unquoted versions are subject to word splitting
$* preserves quotes while $@ does not
$@ only works inside functions; $* works everywhere
correct_answer: "$@" preserves each argument as a separate quoted word (3 args); "$*" combines all arguments into a single string; unquoted versions are subject to word splitting

question: You need to replace all occurrences of 'old_server' with 'new_server' in all .conf files under /etc/ recursively, creating backups of originals. Which command achieves this safely?
options: grep -r 'old_server' /etc/*.conf | sed 's/old_server/new_server/g'
find /etc -name '*.conf' -exec sed -i.bak 's/old_server/new_server/g' {} +
replace old_server new_server /etc/*.conf
awk '{gsub(old_server, new_server)}' /etc/*.conf
correct_answer: find /etc -name '*.conf' -exec sed -i.bak 's/old_server/new_server/g' {} +

question: What does 'set -euo pipefail' at the top of a bash script do, and why is it considered best practice?
options: It enables verbose output for debugging
-e exits on any command failure; -u treats unset variables as errors; -o pipefail causes a pipeline to fail if any command in the pipe fails (not just the last one)
It sets environment variables for Unicode support
It enables parallel execution of commands in the script
correct_answer: -e exits on any command failure; -u treats unset variables as errors; -o pipefail causes a pipeline to fail if any command in the pipe fails (not just the last one)

question: You need to monitor a log file and trigger an alert when more than 10 'ERROR' lines appear within any 60-second window. Which approach is most suitable?
options: tail -f /var/log/app.log | grep ERROR | mail admin
Use 'tail -f' piped to an awk script that tracks timestamps and counts ERRORs within a rolling 60s window, triggering an alert command when threshold exceeds 10
cron job every minute running 'grep -c ERROR /var/log/app.log'
watch -n 1 'cat /var/log/app.log | grep ERROR'
correct_answer: Use 'tail -f' piped to an awk script that tracks timestamps and counts ERRORs within a rolling 60s window, triggering an alert command when threshold exceeds 10

question: In a bash script, what is the difference between 'exec > /var/log/script.log 2>&1' and simply redirecting individual commands?
options: There is no difference; both achieve the same result
'exec' replaces the shell's own stdout/stderr file descriptors, so ALL subsequent output in the script goes to the log file without needing per-command redirects
'exec' runs the script in a subshell
'exec' only redirects errors, not standard output
correct_answer: 'exec' replaces the shell's own stdout/stderr file descriptors, so ALL subsequent output in the script goes to the log file without needing per-command redirects

question: A production cgroup-v2 setup limits a container to 2 CPUs and 4GB RAM. The application inside reports seeing 32 CPUs and 64GB RAM from /proc/cpuinfo and /proc/meminfo. Why?
options: cgroups are not working correctly; restart the container runtime
/proc/cpuinfo and /proc/meminfo reflect the host's hardware, not cgroup limits; the application should use cgroup-aware APIs or files like /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max
The container has broken out of its cgroup constraints
cgroup-v2 does not support CPU limits
correct_answer: /proc/cpuinfo and /proc/meminfo reflect the host's hardware, not cgroup limits; the application should use cgroup-aware APIs or files like /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max

question: What are Linux namespaces, and which namespace types are used by containers to provide isolation?
options: Namespaces are DNS zones; containers use them for name resolution

Namespaces isolate kernel resources per process group: PID (process IDs), NET (network stack), MNT (mount points), UTS (hostname), IPC (inter-process communication), USER (UID/GID mapping), and CGROUP
Namespaces are only used by KVM virtual machines, not containers
Namespaces are filesystem partitions assigned to each container
correct_answer: Namespaces isolate kernel resources per process group: PID (process IDs), NET (network stack), MNT (mount points), UTS (hostname), IPC (inter-process communication), USER (UID/GID mapping), and CGROUP

question: You SSH into a server and find a process using 100% of a single CPU core. 'top' shows it as a zombie process (Z state). What does this mean and how do you clean it up?
options: Kill the zombie with 'kill -9 <zombie-pid>'

A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been collected by its parent; kill the parent process or send SIGCHLD to the parent to reap it — you cannot kill a zombie directly since it's already dead
Reboot the server; zombies cannot be removed otherwise
Use 'renice -20 <pid>' to reduce its CPU usage
correct_answer: A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been collected by its parent; kill the parent process or send SIGCHLD to the parent to reap it — you cannot kill a zombie directly since it's already dead

question: What is the difference between SIGTERM (signal 15), SIGKILL (signal 9), and SIGHUP (signal 1)?
options: All three signals immediately terminate the process in the same way
SIGTERM requests graceful termination (can be caught/handled); SIGKILL forces immediate termination (cannot be caught); SIGHUP traditionally signals config reload or terminal hangup (can be caught)
SIGTERM is for terminals; SIGKILL is for kernel processes; SIGHUP is for network connections
SIGKILL is the gentlest signal; SIGTERM is the harshest
correct_answer: SIGTERM requests graceful termination (can be caught/handled); SIGKILL forces immediate termination (cannot be caught); SIGHUP traditionally signals config reload or terminal hangup (can be caught)

question: You need to run a long-running process that survives after you disconnect from SSH. What are the differences between nohup, screen, tmux, and disown?
options: They are all identical tools

nohup prevents SIGHUP on disconnect but provides no reattach; screen/tmux create persistent terminal sessions you can reattach to; disown removes a running job from the shell's job table so it won't receive SIGHUP
screen is deprecated in favor of nohup on all modern systems
tmux only works on Ubuntu; screen only works on CentOS
correct_answer: nohup prevents SIGHUP on disconnect but provides no reattach; screen/tmux create persistent terminal sessions you can reattach to; disown removes a running job from the shell's job table so it won't receive SIGHUP

question: What does the 'nice' value of a process control, and what is the range of nice values on Linux?
options: Nice controls I/O priority; range is 0 to 100
Nice controls CPU scheduling priority; range is -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest priority); only root can set negative nice values
Nice controls memory allocation priority; range is 1 to 10
Nice controls network bandwidth; range is -100 to 100
correct_answer: Nice controls CPU scheduling priority; range is -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest priority); only root can set negative nice values

question: A process forks thousands of child processes rapidly, causing the system to become unresponsive (fork bomb). How can you prevent this permanently?
options: Set 'MaxProcesses=100' in /etc/sysctl.conf
Set per-user process limits in /etc/security/limits.conf (e.g., 'username hard nproc 500') or use systemd's LimitNPROC
Disable the fork() system call in the kernel
Run 'ulimit -u 100' once as root; it persists across reboots
correct_answer: Set per-user process limits in /etc/security/limits.conf (e.g., 'username hard nproc 500') or use systemd's LimitNPROC

question: You need to set up an NFS mount from a remote server at 10.0.1.50:/shared/data to local /mnt/data with read-write access. The mount must persist across reboots and use NFSv4. What /etc/fstab entry is correct?
options: 10.0.1.50:/shared/data /mnt/data nfs4 rw,hard,intr 0 0
nfs://10.0.1.50/shared/data /mnt/data auto defaults 0 0
//10.0.1.50/shared/data /mnt/data cifs rw 0 0
mount -t nfs 10.0.1.50:/shared/data /mnt/data
correct_answer: 10.0.1.50:/shared/data /mnt/data nfs4 rw,hard,intr 0 0

question: What is the difference between a hard link and a soft (symbolic) link in Linux?
options: Hard links work across filesystems; soft links do not

A hard link is a directory entry pointing to the same inode (same data blocks); a soft link is a special file containing the path to the target — it breaks if the target is moved/deleted, and works across filesystems
Soft links share the same inode as the target; hard links create a copy
There is no practical difference between them
correct_answer: A hard link is a directory entry pointing to the same inode (same data blocks); a soft link is a special file containing the path to the target — it breaks if the target is moved/deleted, and works across filesystems

question: A CentOS server needs to be configured as a DNS client using systemd-resolved. After changing /etc/resolv.conf, it reverts on reboot. How should DNS servers be persistently configured?
options: Edit /etc/resolv.conf with the immutable attribute (chattr +i)
Configure DNS settings in /etc/systemd/resolved.conf or via nmcli for NetworkManager-managed interfaces, as systemd-resolved regenerates resolv.conf
Add DNS entries to /etc/hosts instead
Use 'echo nameserver 8.8.8.8 >> /etc/resolv.conf' in rc.local
correct_answer: Configure DNS settings in /etc/systemd/resolved.conf or via nmcli for NetworkManager-managed interfaces, as systemd-resolved regenerates resolv.conf

question: What is the purpose of the /proc/sys/net/core/somaxconn parameter, and when would you increase it?
options: It controls the maximum number of open files per process
It defines the maximum length of the listen backlog queue for sockets; increase it for high-connection-rate servers (e.g., web servers) to prevent connection drops during traffic spikes
It sets the maximum number of network interfaces
It controls the DNS cache timeout
correct_answer: It defines the maximum length of the listen backlog queue for sockets; increase it for high-connection-rate servers (e.g., web servers) to prevent connection drops during traffic spikes

question: You need to allow only IP ranges 10.0.0.0/8 and 192.168.1.0/24 to access port 443, and block all other inbound traffic. Using iptables, what is the correct rule sequence?
options: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -s 10.0.0.0/8 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A INPUT -s 192.168.1.0/24 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP
iptables -A INPUT -j DROP; iptables -A INPUT -s 10.0.0.0/8 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -s 10.0.0.0/8 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
correct_answer: iptables -A INPUT -s 10.0.0.0/8 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A INPUT -s 192.168.1.0/24 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

question: What is the purpose of Linux kernel parameter 'net.ipv4.ip_forward=1'?
options: It enables the system to forward ICMP echo requests only
It enables IP packet forwarding between network interfaces, turning the system into a router; required for NAT, VPN gateways, and container networking
It forwards all traffic to localhost
It enables IPv6 compatibility mode
correct_answer: It enables IP packet forwarding between network interfaces, turning the system into a router; required for NAT, VPN gateways, and container networking

question: You're troubleshooting intermittent 'connection refused' errors to a service on port 8080. 'ss -tlnp' confirms the service is listening. 'conntrack -L' shows the conntrack table is full. What is likely happening?
options: The service process has crashed
The kernel's connection tracking table (nf_conntrack) is full, causing new connections to be dropped; increase net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max
Port 8080 is blocked by SELinux
The client is using the wrong protocol
correct_answer: The kernel's connection tracking table (nf_conntrack) is full, causing new connections to be dropped; increase net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max

question: You need to automate a task that runs every 15 minutes during business hours (9 AM to 6 PM) on weekdays only. What is the correct crontab entry?
options: */15 * * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh
*/15 9-17 * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh
0,15,30,45 9-18 * * * /path/to/script.sh
15 9-18 * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh
correct_answer: */15 9-17 * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh

question: What is the difference between cron and systemd timers? When would you prefer one over the other?
options: They are identical; systemd timers are just a wrapper around cron
Systemd timers offer better logging (journalctl), dependency management, on-boot scheduling, monotonic timers, and can trigger on events; cron is simpler but lacks these features
Cron supports millisecond precision; systemd timers do not
Systemd timers only work for system services, not user scripts
correct_answer: Systemd timers offer better logging (journalctl), dependency management, on-boot scheduling, monotonic timers, and can trigger on events; cron is simpler but lacks these features

question: After a kernel upgrade, the system fails to boot with 'Kernel panic - not syncing: VFS: Unable to mount root fs'. What is the most likely cause?
options: The hard drive has failed
The initramfs/initrd was not generated for the new kernel, so the root filesystem driver or storage drivers are not available during early boot
The bootloader is corrupted
The root password was changed
correct_answer: The initramfs/initrd was not generated for the new kernel, so the root filesystem driver or storage drivers are not available during early boot

question: A system boots to a GRUB rescue prompt. How do you boot into single-user mode for recovery?
options: Type 'rescue' at the GRUB prompt
Edit the GRUB boot entry (press 'e'), find the 'linux' line, append 'single' or 'init=/bin/bash' or 'systemd.unit=rescue.target', then boot with Ctrl+X
Press F8 repeatedly during boot
Boot from a USB drive and run 'grub-repair'
correct_answer: Edit the GRUB boot entry (press 'e'), find the 'linux' line, append 'single' or 'init=/bin/bash' or 'systemd.unit=rescue.target', then boot with Ctrl+X

question: What is the correct order of the Linux boot process?
options: BIOS/UEFI → Kernel → Bootloader → Init → Userspace
BIOS/UEFI → Bootloader (GRUB) → Kernel + initramfs → Init system (systemd/init) → Userspace services
Bootloader → BIOS → Kernel → Filesystem → Network
UEFI → Filesystem → Kernel → GRUB → Systemd
correct_answer: BIOS/UEFI → Bootloader (GRUB) → Kernel + initramfs → Init system (systemd/init) → Userspace services

question: A colleague ran 'chmod -R 777 /' on a production server before being stopped. Beyond the obvious security implications, which critical system functionality will break?
options: Only web server files are affected

SSH will refuse to work (sshd requires strict permissions on /etc/ssh/), sudo may fail, cron jobs may not run, and any SUID/SGID binaries lose their special bits — effectively breaking authentication and privilege escalation
The system will immediately kernel panic
Only user home directories are affected
correct_answer: SSH will refuse to work (sshd requires strict permissions on /etc/ssh/), sudo may fail, cron jobs may not run, and any SUID/SGID binaries lose their special bits — effectively breaking authentication and privilege escalation

question: You need to configure PAM to enforce password complexity: minimum 12 characters, at least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 digit, and 1 special character. Which PAM module and configuration achieves this?
options: pam_securetty.so with min_length=12
pam_pwquality.so (or pam_cracklib.so) with minlen=12 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 in /etc/pam.d/common-password or /etc/security/pwquality.conf
pam_access.so with password_rules=complex
pam_limits.so with password_length=12
correct_answer: pam_pwquality.so (or pam_cracklib.so) with minlen=12 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 in /etc/pam.d/common-password or /etc/security/pwquality.conf

question: What does the command 'grep -rn --include='*.py' 'import os' /opt/app/' do?
options: Recursively searches all files under /opt/app for 'import os'
Recursively searches only .py files under /opt/app/ for 'import os', showing file names and line numbers
Searches only the first .py file found for 'import os'
Creates a new file containing all Python imports
correct_answer: Recursively searches only .py files under /opt/app/ for 'import os', showing file names and line numbers

question: You need to analyze an Apache access log and find the top 10 IP addresses by request count. Which command pipeline achieves this?
options: cat access.log | sort | head -10
awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
grep -c IP access.log | head -10
tail -10 access.log | cut -d' ' -f1
correct_answer: awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

question: What is the difference between /dev/null, /dev/zero, and /dev/urandom?
options: They are all identical devices that discard data
/dev/null discards all data written to it; /dev/zero produces an infinite stream of zero bytes (for disk wiping, file creation); /dev/urandom provides cryptographically secure pseudorandom bytes
/dev/null produces random data; /dev/zero discards data; /dev/urandom produces zeros
/dev/zero is for zeroing network connections; /dev/null is for zeroing disks
correct_answer: /dev/null discards all data written to it; /dev/zero produces an infinite stream of zero bytes (for disk wiping, file creation); /dev/urandom provides cryptographically secure pseudorandom bytes

question: Explain the difference between /etc/environment, /etc/profile, ~/.bashrc, and ~/.bash_profile. In which order are they sourced for a login shell?
options: They are all sourced simultaneously

For a login shell: /etc/environment (PAM) → /etc/profile → /etc/profile.d/*.sh → ~/.bash_profile (or ~/.profile); ~/.bashrc is sourced only for non-login interactive shells (but often called from .bash_profile)
~/.bashrc is always sourced first, then /etc/profile
/etc/environment is only for graphical sessions
correct_answer: For a login shell: /etc/environment (PAM) → /etc/profile → /etc/profile.d/*.sh → ~/.bash_profile (or ~/.profile); ~/.bashrc is sourced only for non-login interactive shells (but often called from .bash_profile)

question: You run 'sysctl -a | grep net.core.rmem_max' and it shows 212992 (208KB). Your application requires large UDP receive buffers of 4MB. What do you need to do?
options: Increase the application's thread count to handle packets faster
Set net.core.rmem_max=4194304 via sysctl and configure the application to request the larger buffer with SO_RCVBUF
Install a faster network card
Change the MTU to 9000 to increase buffer size
correct_answer: Set net.core.rmem_max=4194304 via sysctl and configure the application to request the larger buffer with SO_RCVBUF

question: What does the 'ldd' command do, and why should you never run it on untrusted binaries?
options: ldd lists disk drive partitions; it's safe to run on any file
ldd lists shared library dependencies of a binary; on some systems it may actually execute the binary's dynamic linker, potentially running malicious code in untrusted binaries
ldd checks for logical disk damage; it's always safe
ldd lists directory contents in detail; security is not a concern
correct_answer: ldd lists shared library dependencies of a binary; on some systems it may actually execute the binary's dynamic linker, potentially running malicious code in untrusted binaries

question: A server's /var/log/messages shows 'kernel: TCP: request_sock_TCP: Possible SYN flooding on port 80. Sending cookies.' What is happening and what tuning can help?
options: The server is under a SYN flood attack or experiencing high legitimate connection rates; net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1 (already enabled) helps, and increasing net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog and net.core.somaxconn can handle more concurrent half-open connections
The HTTP server is misconfigured and sending bad responses
Port 80 is blocked by the firewall
The server needs more RAM to handle TCP connections
correct_answer: The server is under a SYN flood attack or experiencing high legitimate connection rates; net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1 (already enabled) helps, and increasing net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog and net.core.somaxconn can handle more concurrent half-open connections

question: You need to check if a remote server's port 5432 is reachable from your machine but nc/ncat/telnet are not installed. Which alternative approaches can you use?
options: There is no way to test TCP connectivity without nc or telnet
Use 'bash -c echo >/dev/tcp/server/5432' (bash built-in), 'curl -v telnet://server:5432', or 'python3 -c "import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.connect(('server',5432))"'
Use 'ping server:5432' to test the port
Use 'traceroute -p 5432 server' (traceroute tests ICMP, not TCP ports)
correct_answer: Use 'bash -c echo >/dev/tcp/server/5432' (bash built-in), 'curl -v telnet://server:5432', or 'python3 -c "import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.connect(('server',5432))"'

question: What is the difference between a 'bind mount' and a regular mount in Linux? When would you use a bind mount?
options: A bind mount encrypts the mounted directory; a regular mount does not

A bind mount makes an existing directory tree visible at another mount point (same filesystem, no block device); useful for exposing host paths into chroot/container environments or creating alternative access points
A bind mount is faster than a regular mount because it skips filesystem checks
Bind mounts only work with NFS filesystems
correct_answer: A bind mount makes an existing directory tree visible at another mount point (same filesystem, no block device); useful for exposing host paths into chroot/container environments or creating alternative access points

question: You run 'perf top' on a production server and notice a kernel function '__do_page_fault' consuming 25% CPU. What does this indicate?
options: The disk is failing with bad sectors
The system is experiencing excessive page faults, likely due to memory pressure, large memory-mapped files, or an application accessing memory patterns that cause frequent TLB misses
The CPU is overheating and throttling
The network stack is processing too many packets
correct_answer: The system is experiencing excessive page faults, likely due to memory pressure, large memory-mapped files, or an application accessing memory patterns that cause frequent TLB misses

question: What is the purpose of the /etc/nsswitch.conf file, and what does 'hosts: files dns myhostname' mean?
options: It configures NFS switch parameters

nsswitch.conf controls the order of name resolution sources; 'hosts: files dns myhostname' means: first check /etc/hosts, then DNS servers, then the local hostname — this ordering determines how hostnames are resolved
It configures network switch VLAN settings
It controls which users can access DNS services
correct_answer: nsswitch.conf controls the order of name resolution sources; 'hosts: files dns myhostname' means: first check /etc/hosts, then DNS servers, then the local hostname — this ordering determines how hostnames are resolved

question: A server experiences a sudden disk performance drop. 'smartctl -a /dev/sda' shows 'Reallocated_Sector_Ct' increasing and 'Current_Pending_Sector' at 48. What action should you take?
options: Run fsck to repair the sectors
The disk is developing bad sectors and likely failing; plan an immediate replacement, ensure backups are current, and consider putting the RAID array into a degraded state for proactive swap
Increase the disk's write cache to improve performance
Reformat the disk to fix the bad sectors
correct_answer: The disk is developing bad sectors and likely failing; plan an immediate replacement, ensure backups are current, and consider putting the RAID array into a degraded state for proactive swap

question: You need to configure log rotation for an application that writes to /var/log/myapp/app.log. The requirements are: rotate daily, keep 30 days, compress rotated files, and signal the application to reopen log files after rotation. Which logrotate configuration achieves this?
options: /var/log/myapp/app.log { yearly rotate 1 }
/var/log/myapp/app.log { daily rotate 30 compress delaycompress missingok notifempty postrotate /usr/bin/systemctl reload myapp endscript }
/var/log/myapp/app.log { monthly rotate 30 nocompress }
crontab: 0 0 * * * gzip /var/log/myapp/app.log
correct_answer: /var/log/myapp/app.log { daily rotate 30 compress delaycompress missingok notifempty postrotate /usr/bin/systemctl reload myapp endscript }

question: What is the difference between TCP keepalive and HTTP keep-alive? How do you tune TCP keepalive on Linux?
options: They are the same thing; both keep connections open

TCP keepalive is a kernel-level mechanism that sends probes on idle TCP connections to detect dead peers (tuned via net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time/intvl/probes); HTTP keep-alive reuses a TCP connection for multiple HTTP requests — they operate at different layers
TCP keepalive only works on UDP connections
HTTP keep-alive replaces TCP keepalive on modern systems
correct_answer: TCP keepalive is a kernel-level mechanism that sends probes on idle TCP connections to detect dead peers (tuned via net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time/intvl/probes); HTTP keep-alive reuses a TCP connection for multiple HTTP requests — they operate at different layers

question: You need to audit all successful and failed login attempts on a server. Which log files and commands would you use?
options: Only check /var/log/messages

Check /var/log/auth.log (Debian/Ubuntu) or /var/log/secure (RHEL/CentOS) for SSH/PAM entries; use 'last' for successful logins, 'lastb' for failed logins, and 'journalctl -u sshd' for systemd-based systems
Run 'who -a' to see all historical logins
Check /etc/passwd for login records
correct_answer: Check /var/log/auth.log (Debian/Ubuntu) or /var/log/secure (RHEL/CentOS) for SSH/PAM entries; use 'last' for successful logins, 'lastb' for failed logins, and 'journalctl -u sshd' for systemd-based systems

question: A team member ran 'iptables -F' (flush all rules) on a production server that had a default DROP policy. What happened and how do you recover?
options: Nothing happened; the default policy ensures traffic still flows

All custom ACCEPT rules were removed but the default DROP policy remained, so ALL traffic (including SSH) is now blocked; recover via out-of-band access (console/IPMI/BMC), then restore the iptables ruleset
Only ICMP traffic was affected
The server automatically reverts to ACCEPT policy after a flush
correct_answer: All custom ACCEPT rules were removed but the default DROP policy remained, so ALL traffic (including SSH) is now blocked; recover via out-of-band access (console/IPMI/BMC), then restore the iptables ruleset

question: What is the purpose of the /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern setting, and how would you configure it to capture core dumps for debugging production crashes?
options: It controls the CPU core affinity pattern for processes

It defines the filename pattern and location for core dump files when a process crashes; set it to a path like '/var/coredumps/core.%e.%p.%t' to capture dumps with executable name, PID, and timestamp — also ensure ulimit -c is set to unlimited
It controls how kernel modules are loaded
It sets the pattern for kernel boot parameters
correct_answer: It defines the filename pattern and location for core dump files when a process crashes; set it to a path like '/var/coredumps/core.%e.%p.%t' to capture dumps with executable name, PID, and timestamp — also ensure ulimit -c is set to unlimited

question: You need to transfer a 50GB database dump between two servers. The network bandwidth is 1Gbps but rsync is only achieving 200Mbps. What could improve transfer speed?
options: Use scp instead; it's always faster than rsync

rsync's SSH encryption adds overhead; try rsync with --compress disabled (already compressed data), use a faster cipher (-e 'ssh -c aes128-gcm@openssh.com'), or use rsync daemon mode (no SSH) on a trusted network
Increase the server's RAM to 128GB
Use 'cp' command over NFS; it's faster than rsync
correct_answer: rsync's SSH encryption adds overhead; try rsync with --compress disabled (already compressed data), use a faster cipher (-e 'ssh -c aes128-gcm@openssh.com'), or use rsync daemon mode (no SSH) on a trusted network

question: What is the difference between 'useradd' and 'adduser' on Debian/Ubuntu systems?
options: They are exactly the same command

'useradd' is a low-level binary that creates users with minimal defaults (no home dir unless -m is specified); 'adduser' is a higher-level Perl script that interactively creates users with home directory, shell, and password prompts
'adduser' is deprecated in favor of 'useradd'
'useradd' creates system users; 'adduser' creates regular users only
correct_answer: 'useradd' is a low-level binary that creates users with minimal defaults (no home dir unless -m is specified); 'adduser' is a higher-level Perl script that interactively creates users with home directory, shell, and password prompts

question: A security policy requires that user 'deployer' can only run '/usr/bin/systemctl restart myapp' and '/usr/bin/systemctl status myapp' via sudo, with no password prompt. What sudoers entry achieves this?
options: deployer ALL=(ALL) ALL
deployer ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl restart myapp, /usr/bin/systemctl status myapp
deployer ALL=(root) PASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl *
deployer ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL
correct_answer: deployer ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl restart myapp, /usr/bin/systemctl status myapp

question: What is the difference between ACLs (Access Control Lists) and traditional Unix permissions? When would you use ACLs?
options: ACLs are identical to Unix permissions but use different syntax

ACLs extend Unix permissions by allowing fine-grained access for multiple specific users and groups on a single file — useful when the owner/group/other model is insufficient, e.g., granting read access to two different groups
ACLs only work on NFS filesystems
ACLs replace Unix permissions entirely; you can only use one system
correct_answer: ACLs extend Unix permissions by allowing fine-grained access for multiple specific users and groups on a single file — useful when the owner/group/other model is insufficient, e.g., granting read access to two different groups
